description:
You are a data-science researcher who is about to share several private
    datasets with collaborators through an SSH-based data server.  
    Before you start, you want to:

    1. Create a brand-new ed25519 SSH key-pair that will be used
       exclusively for dataset transfers.  
    2. Make sure the public key is ready for server-side installation by
       placing it in your personal <authorized_keys> file (so that you
       can test locally right away).  
    3. Leave behind a tiny, two-line log file so that your lab’s
       automation scripts can pick it up later.

    Very important implementation details:

    • Key filenames  
      – Private key:  /home/user/.ssh/dataset_ed25519  
      – Public key:   /home/user/.ssh/dataset_ed25519.pub  

    • Key comment (must be included when you generate the key):  
      researcher_dataset_access

    • Log file  
      – Path:  /home/user/ssh_setup.log  
      – Format (EXACTLY two lines, no extra spaces or trailing blanks):  
            KeyFile: /home/user/.ssh/dataset_ed25519  
            PublicKeyAddedToAuthorizedKeys: yes  

      The automated test will look for those two lines verbatim, in that
      order.

    • authorised_keys handling  
      – The contents of /home/user/.ssh/dataset_ed25519.pub must be
        appended to /home/user/.ssh/authorized_keys  
      – After you finish, the comment string
        “researcher_dataset_access” must appear *exactly once* in
        /home/user/.ssh/authorized_keys.

    • Permissions  
      – ~/.ssh must be chmod 700 (or tighter)  
      – The private key file must be chmod 600.  

    Your job: from a blank terminal prompt, carry out the above steps
    (typically this is only two or three commands).  When you are done,
    both the key-pair and the log file should exist exactly as specified.
truth:
The grader will verify that, by the end of the session:

    1. Directory /home/user/.ssh exists and has permissions 700 or
       stricter (octal ≤0700).

    2. File /home/user/.ssh/dataset_ed25519  
       – exists, is non-empty, and permissions are 600 (octal ≤0600).

    3. File /home/user/.ssh/dataset_ed25519.pub  
       – exists, is non-empty, and contains the literal text  
         “researcher_dataset_access” (including the underscores).  

    4. File /home/user/.ssh/authorized_keys  
       – exists, is non-empty;  
       – contains exactly one occurrence of the string  
         “researcher_dataset_access”;  
       – that occurrence is on the same line as an ssh-ed25519 public-key
         block (i.e., the line starts with “ssh-ed25519 ”).  

    5. Log file /home/user/ssh_setup.log  
       – exists, is plain text;  
       – consists of exactly two newline-separated lines:  
            KeyFile: /home/user/.ssh/dataset_ed25519  
            PublicKeyAddedToAuthorizedKeys: yes  

    Any deviation from the above, including extra whitespace at EOL,
    additional lines, or missing comment strings, will cause the test to
    fail.
